title: Non-uniqueness in the Leray-Hopf class for a dyadic Navier-Stokes model

def:1.1.summary: Leray-Hopf solution of the Obukhov dyadic model: u(t) in $L_t^\infty L^2$ cap $L_t^2 H^1$ satisfying the energy inequality (1.4) for all t.

thm:1.2.summary: For $\alpha$ in (2,4) and sufficiently large $\lambda$, there exists initial data $u^0$ in $cap_{s<\alpha-2} H^s$ giving rise to two distinct Leray-Hopf solutions of the Obukhov dyadic model.

rem:1.3.summary: The lower bound $\alpha > 2$ is sharp (subcritical regime has uniqueness). The upper bound $\alpha < 4$ is technical and can be removed by refined analysis.

rem:1.4.summary: Both solutions u, v are non-negative and decay exponentially in frequency for t > 0, corresponding to spatially smooth Navier-Stokes solutions.

rem:1.5.summary: Large $\lambda$ is physically meaningful in the Obukhov model context.

rem:1.6.summary: For large $\lambda$, the data is nearly discretely self-similar under $\lambda^n$ rescalings. Non-uniqueness arises from the two possible ways the symmetry can break for positive times (invariance under $\lambda^{2n}$ only).

rem:1.7.summary: The initial data lies in every supercritical $H^s$ and in the critical space $X^{\alpha-2}$, corresponding to velocity in $B_{p,\infty}^{-1+d/p}$ at maximum intermittency.

rem:1.8.summary: The non-uniqueness scenario is unstable: does not persist for an open set of data. A destabilizing drift term dominates the dissipation on the relevant time scale.

prop:2.1.summary: Constructs solutions on $[t_0$, $\infty)$ via Banach fixed point in a weighted norm space Y. For $\sigma$ in {1,2} and admissible delays $\tau_k$, produces a solution with exponentially decaying bounds (2.2) on paired modes $(u_{k-1}, u_k)$.

lem:3.1.summary: Translates the solution from Prop 2.1 into (r,z) coordinates: shows (r,z) in $B_{\sigma,[t_0,\infty)}(10)$ and gives improved bound on $z_k(t_0)$ with $O(exp(-3N_k^2(t_0-\tau_k)))$ error.

def:3.2.summary: Weak bootstrap hypothesis H(t): solution exists on [t, $\infty)$, agrees with Prop 2.1 on $[t_0$, $\infty)$, and (r,z) in $B_{\sigma,[t,t_0]}(10)$.

def:3.3.summary: Strong bootstrap hypothesis H'(t): strengthens H(t) by requiring (r,z) in $B_{\sigma,[t,t_0]}(1/100)$.

prop:3.4.summary: Self-improving bounds: if u satisfies $H(t_1)$ then it also satisfies $H'(t_1)$. Proved by Duhamel estimates showing the weak bounds bootstrap to 1/100-scale control via exponential decay and large $\lambda$.

prop:3.5.summary: Propagation backwards: if $H'(t_1)$ holds for $t_1 > 0$, there exists $t_2 < t_1$ with $H(t_2)$. Uses contraction mapping on the backward-in-time Duhamel formulation. Also proves backward uniqueness.

prop:3.6.summary: The set {t in $[0,t_0]$ : H'(t) holds} is closed. Proved by uniform bounds on r-dot, z-dot and their derivatives near the limit point, giving $C^1$ extensions.

prop:3.7.summary: For any $\mu$ in M, constructs two non-negative solutions u, v of the Obukhov model on [0, $\infty)$ with prescribed (r,z) bounds (3.11), instantaneous smoothing (3.12), and matching zeroth mode $u_0(0) = v_0(0)$.

lem:A.1.summary: Gronwall-type inequalities: (i) forward integral inequality with decay $\delta$ implies g lesssim $\delta$; (ii) backward integral inequality with $g(t_0)^{t/t_0}$ seed implies polynomial-in-time growth bound.

lem:A.2.summary: Technical continuity facts: (i) f: A $\to X^s$ is continuous and bounded A $\to X^0$; $(ii) \mu \to (r(t),z(t))$ is continuous M $\to (X^s)^2$ for each t > 0; $(iii) (r(t),z(t)) \to (r^0,z^0)$ uniformly in $\mu$ as t $\to 0+$.

edges.note: Proof of Theorem 1.2 uses Prop 3.7 to construct the mapping $\mu \to (u,v)$.
Uses asymptotics from Prop 2.1 to show u and v are distinct for large t.
Lemma A.2(i)-(iii) used to show T: M $\to$ M is continuous, enabling Schauder fixed point.
Energy inequality (1.4) from def:1.1 verified via truncated energy balance and Fatou's lemma.
Applies Prop 2.1 twice $(\sigma=1$ and $\sigma=2)$ to construct u and v on $[t_0$, $\infty)$.
Props 3.4 + 3.5 together make {t : H'(t)} open; used in the bootstrap argument to propagate to t=0.
Props 3.4 + 3.5 together make {t : H'(t)} open via Tao's Prop 1.21 bootstrap recipe.
Closedness of {t : H'(t)} needed to complete the connectedness bootstrap argument.
Lemma 3.1 provides initial condition $H'(t_0)$ to seed the bootstrap.
Bounds from Prop 2.1 enter Duhamel estimates for $r_k$ and $z_k$ on $[t_1, t_0]$.
Assumes hypothesis $H(t_1)$ as input.
Conclusion is $H'(t_1)$: the weak bounds self-improve to strong bounds.
Uses $z_k(t_0)$ estimate from Lemma 3.1 in the $J_1$ linear term bound.
Assumes strong hypothesis $H'(t_1)$ as input.
Produces $H(t_2)$ for some $t_2 < t_1$ via contraction mapping.
Needs $H'(t_1)$ bounds (from self-improvement) as data for the backward contraction.
Uses backward uniqueness from Prop 3.5 to show solutions from decreasing $t_i$ agree on overlaps.
Translates $(u_{k-1}, u_k)$ bounds from Prop 2.1 into $(r_k, z_k)$ coordinates via Taylor expansion.
Parts (ii)-(iii) use the solution mapping from Prop 3.7 to establish continuity of $\mu \to (r^0, z^0)$.
Discusses sharpness of the $\alpha$ range in Theorem 1.2.
Interprets the symmetry-breaking mechanism behind non-uniqueness in Theorem 1.2.

layer_1_setup.description: Definitions and the main non-uniqueness theorem statement

layer_2_stable_manifold.description: Construction of solutions on $[t_0$, $\infty)$ via contraction mapping on paired-mode Duhamel system

layer_3_bootstrap_machinery.description: Bootstrap hypotheses and the three propositions (self-improvement, backward propagation, closedness) that extend solutions to t=0

layer_4_solution_construction.description: Full solution construction for arbitrary $\mu$ in M, producing two solutions with matching zeroth mode

layer_5_fixed_point.description: Schauder fixed point on the parameter space M to find $\mu$ such that $u^0 = v^0$, completing the non-uniqueness proof

layer_6_technical_appendix.description: Gronwall-type estimates and continuity lemmas supporting the fixed point argument
